text: This video demonstrates a case of laparoscopic splenectomy for a vascular splenic mass. The patient is a thirty-two-year-old male who presented to the emergency department three days after blunt abdominal trauma with diffuse abdominal pain more in the right upper quadrant. The patient has no significant past medical or past surgical history. The physical exam was significant for tenderness to palpation over the right middle and inferior chest wall with mild tenderness to palpation in the right upper quadrant. He had no left upper quadrant tenderness. His labs were within normal limits. His abdominal CT scan showed a splenic mass which was confirmed by MRI to be a vascular mass suggestive of a vascular tumor. The patient was scheduled for an elective surgery and received immunizations against pneumococcus and Haemophilus influenzae type B two weeks before surgery. In the operating room, the patient was placed in the right lateral decubitus position and a total of four ports were used as depicted in the diagram. The sequence of surgery was as indicated in this slide.

The surgeon frees the inferior pole of the spleen from adhesions and takes down the inferior polar vessels. The superior pole is freed by initially taking down the superior short gastric vessels, followed by the complete takedown of the remaining short gastric vessels and superior polar vessels. The hilum is dissected, isolating the splenic artery and vein, with the splenic artery showing magistral distribution, dividing near the hilum.

The surgeon uses a vascular stapler device to transect each vessel separately, ensuring good hemostasis while carefully avoiding injury to the pancreas during dissection in the hilar area. The spleen is mobilized by freeing its attachments, including the splenocolic, splenorenal, and splenophrenic ligaments. The splenic mass is visible and protected from injury during this maneuver. Once the spleen is free, it is placed in an endobag and extracted after partial marsupialization. Minimal irrigation is performed, and hemostasis is confirmed. A zero-vicral suture is used to close the fascia of the left subcostal incision.

The final pathology revealed organizing splenic hematoma. In conclusion, laparoscopic splenectomy for vascular splenic mass can be performed safely. Understanding the anatomical variation of the splenic artery distribution is an important aspect during this procedure to minimize the blood loss. In a follow-up eight months after surgery, the patient is doing well.
